content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 203–204 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_006_page_001.jpg, DEU_006_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 148

---

1 And this [is] the commandment, the statutes and the ordinances that the LORD your God commanded to teach you to do in the land into which you are crossing over to possess it —

2 so that you will fear the LORD your God, to keep all his statutes and his commandments that I am commanding you — you, and your son, and your son's son — all the days of your life, and so that your days may be lengthened.

3 And you shall hear, O Yisra'el, and you shall take heed to do [it], so that it may go well with you and so that you may greatly multiply — as the LORD, the God of your fathers, spoke to you — [in] a land flowing with milk and honey.

4 Hear, O Yisra'el: the LORD our God, the LORD [is] one [Shema Yisra'el: YHWH Elohenu YHWH echad].

5 And you shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might [ve-ahavta et YHWH Elohekha be-khol levavkha u-ve-khol nafshekha u-ve-khol me'odekha].

6 And these words that I am commanding you today shall be upon your heart.

7 And you shall repeat them sharply to your sons and shall speak of them when you sit in your house and when you walk on the road and when you lie down and when you rise up.

8 And you shall bind them as a sign upon your hand, and they shall be as frontlets [totafot] between your eyes.

9 And you shall write them upon the doorposts [mezuzot] of your house and upon your gates.

10 And it shall be when the LORD your God brings you to the land that he swore to your fathers — to Avraham, to Yitschak, and to Ya'akov — to give to you: great and good cities that you did not build,

11 and houses full of all good things that you did not fill, and cisterns hewn out that you did not hew, vineyards and olive trees that you did not plant — and you eat and are satisfied —

12 take heed to yourself lest you forget the LORD who brought you out from the land of Mitsrayim, from the house of slaves.

13 The LORD your God you shall fear, and him you shall serve, and by his name you shall swear.

14 You shall not go after other gods, from the gods of the peoples that are around you —

15 for a jealous God [El qanna] [is] the LORD your God in your midst — lest the anger of the LORD your God kindle against you and he destroy you from upon the face of the earth.

16 You shall not test the LORD your God as you tested [him] at Massah.

17 You shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God, and his testimonies and his statutes that he commanded you.

18 And you shall do the right and the good in the eyes of the LORD, so that it may go well with you and you go in and possess the good land that the LORD swore to your fathers —

19 to thrust out all your enemies from before you, as the LORD spoke.

20 When your son asks you tomorrow, saying: "What [are] the testimonies and the statutes and the ordinances that the LORD our God commanded you?" —

21 you shall say to your son: "Slaves we were to Pharaoh in Mitsrayim, and the LORD brought us out from Mitsrayim with a strong hand.

22 And the LORD gave signs and wonders — great and evil — against Mitsrayim, against Pharaoh and against all his house, before our eyes.

23 And us he brought out from there in order to bring us in, to give to us the land that he swore to our fathers.

24 And the LORD commanded us to do all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our good all the days, to keep us alive as [it is] this day.

25 And righteousness it shall be for us when we take heed to do all this commandment before the LORD our God, as he commanded us."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.4 — Shema Yisra'el: YHWH Elohenu YHWH echad:** The *Shema* — the central confession of Jewish faith. The Hebrew is concise to the point of grammatical ambiguity, with no copula. EMET reads: *shema* (hear! — imperative singular addressed to Yisra'el as a collective), *Yisra'el* (vocative), *YHWH Elohenu* (the LORD [is] our God), *YHWH echad* (the LORD [is] one). Four possible interpretations of *echad* (one): (1) numerical uniqueness — there is only one divine being; (2) unity — the LORD is a unified whole; (3) alone — the LORD alone [is God]; (4) *echad* in the sense of "the first, the unique." The LXX (p. 148): *Κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν Κύριος εἷς ἐστιν* — "the LORD our God, the LORD is one" — adding the copula *ἐστιν* (is). The Vaticanus rendering follows MT structure closely. EMET notes that *YHWH Elohenu* can also be parsed as a compound title ("the LORD-our-God"), and *YHWH echad* as the predicate — "the LORD-our-God, the LORD [is] one."

**v.5 — ve-ahavta ("and you shall love"):** The command to love — *ve-ahavta* (Qal perfect with waw-consecutive, functioning as imperative) — is the first and only commandment of Deuteronomy 6 per se. Three prepositional phrases: *be-khol levavkha* (with all your heart), *u-ve-khol nafshekha* (with all your soul), *u-ve-khol me'odekha* (with all your might/very-muchness). The last term *me'od* is an adverb pressed into service as a noun — "your very-much-ness," i.e., your totality, all your resources and energies. The LXX: *ἀγαπήσεις Κύριον τὸν θεόν σου ἐξ ὅλης τῆς διανοίας σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ψυχῆς σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς δυνάμεώς σου* — rendering *lev* as *dianoia* (mind/understanding) rather than *kardia* (heart), and *me'od* as *dynamis* (power/force). The NT citations (Mark 12:30; Luke 10:27) add a fourth element — *dianoia* (mind) — reflecting a different Greek textual tradition.

**v.6 — shall be upon your heart:** *ve-hayu ha-devarim ha-elleh al-levavekha* — literally "and these words shall be upon your heart." Not "in your heart" but *al-levavekha* (upon your heart) — the external written word that rests on the heart, to be absorbed into it. This is a striking spatial image. The LXX: *ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ σου* — "in your heart" — harmonizing away the *al* (upon/on).

**v.7 — you shall repeat them sharply / diligently:** *ve-shinnantam le-vanekha* — the Pi'el of *shanan* (to sharpen, to inculcate, to repeat incisively). The image is of a blade being sharpened — repetition that cuts deep. EMET renders "repeat them sharply." Some modern translations use "impress" or "teach diligently." The LXX: *καὶ προβιβάσεις αὐτὰ τοὺς υἱούς σου* — "and you shall teach/advance these things to your sons" — *probibázo* means to push forward, to lead ahead, a slightly different metaphor.

**v.7 — when you sit... walk... lie down... rise up:** *be-shivtekha... u-ve-lekhtekha... u-ve-shokhbekha... u-ve-kumekha* — four temporal infinitives covering the totality of daily life: in domestic rest, in movement, in sleep, in waking. This is a merism — two pairs of opposites that together encompass all time. The words of Torah should permeate every moment. The LXX (p. 148): *καθήμενος... πορευόμενος... κοιτάζεσθαι... ἀνιστᾶσθαι* — exact four-part structure.

**v.8 — totafot ("frontlets"):** *u-ve-totafot ben einekha* — *totafot* is a hapax legomenon (also at Exod 13:16 and Deut 11:18) with uncertain etymology. The traditional rendering "frontlets" preserves the obscurity. Rabbinic tradition interpreted this as phylacteries (*tefillin*) — small leather boxes containing scripture scrolls bound to the forehead and arm. The LXX: *ἀσάλευτον* (immovable, unshakeable) — a remarkable interpretive translation that reads *totafot* as connoting stability, rather than as a physical object. This is a significant divergence from MT; the Vaticanus (p. 148) preserves *ἀσάλευτον*. EMET preserves the transliteration *totafot* with bracketed gloss [frontlets].

**v.9 — mezuzot ("doorposts"):** *u-khetavtam al-mezuzot beitekha u-vi-she'arekha* — *mezuzah* (pl. *mezuzot*) is simply "doorpost" — the structural element. Rabbinic tradition developed the practice of affixing small parchment scrolls (*mezuzot*) to doorposts in fulfillment of this verse. The literal command is to write the words on the doorpost itself — the object is *devarim* (words/things), not a container. The LXX: *καὶ γράψεις αὐτὰ ἐπὶ τὰς φλιὰς τῶν οἰκιῶν σου καὶ τῶν πυλῶν σου* — exact, using *phliás* (doorposts/thresholds).

**v.11 — cisterns hewn out that you did not hew:** *boroth chatsuvin asher lo-chatsavta* — rock-cut cisterns for water storage, a primary technology of the Cisjordanian highlands. This verse (along with v.10) constitutes a remarkable description of receiving an already-built civilization — cities, houses, cisterns, vineyards, olive groves — all without labor. The gift is also the danger: *lest you forget* (v.12).

**v.13 — by his name you shall swear:** *u-vi-shmo tishava'* — the Qal imperfect of *shava'* (to swear). EMET reads this as a positive command: lawful oaths are to be taken only in the LORD's name. This is in tension with later traditions discouraging all oaths (Matt 5:34); here swearing by YHWH's name is commanded, not forbidden.

**v.16 — at Massah:** *ka'asher nissitem be-Massah* — Massah ("testing") is the name given to the place at Rephidim where the people demanded water and "tested" the LORD (Exod 17:7). The place received the dual name Massah u-Merivah ("Testing and Contention"). Deuteronomy recalls it as the paradigm case of illegitimate testing of God.

**v.20 — your son asks you tomorrow:** *ki yish'alekha vinkha machar* — *machar* (tomorrow) functions idiomatically as "in the future, in time to come." The catechetical setting — a child's question evoking a father's full recitation of salvation history — is the Passover haggadah structure. The LXX: *ἐὰν δὲ ἐρωτήσῃ σε ὁ υἱός σου αὔριον* — exact.

**v.22 — signs and wonders — great and evil:** *otot u-mofetim gedolim ve-ra'im* — "evil" (*ra'im*) here modifies the plagues as terrible, devastating — from the perspective of Mitsrayim. The LXX: *σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα μεγάλα καὶ πονηρά* — great and grievous (*ponera*) — exact.

**v.25 — And righteousness it shall be for us:** *u-tsedaqah tihyeh lanu* — literally "and righteousness it will be for us." Performing the commandments is itself described as *tsedaqah* — an act of right-relationship, of covenant-faithfulness. This is not earning righteousness by works in a merit-based sense, but the keeping of Torah being in itself the expression of a right relationship with the LORD. The LXX: *ἐλεημοσύνη ἔσται ἡμῖν* — "it will be mercy/almsgiving for us." The LXX renders *tsedaqah* as *eleemosyne* (alms/mercy), which shifts the focus from covenantal righteousness to charitable mercy — a notable and theologically freighted divergence. This LXX rendering influenced later Greek-speaking Jewish and early Christian usage of *eleemosyne*.

— EMET
